source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 (split from vector_basics bundle — STATE_4 content + epic_c branch 'equal_vs_parallel_confusion')

real_world_anchor.primary: Two trains on parallel tracks. Both heading north. Equal direction. But if one is slower, their velocity vectors are parallel — not equal.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Two students walking along the school corridor side by side. Same direction. Now imagine one walking faster — still parallel paths, but their velocity vectors are no longer equal.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Two identical arrows drawn at opposite corners of a sheet of paper — same length, same tilt. Position is irrelevant. Both represent the same vector — they are EQUAL.

shared_direction_deg.name: common direction of A and B

formulas.equal_condition: A = B  iff  |A| = |B|  AND  direction(A) = direction(B)
formulas.parallel_condition: A || B  iff  direction(A) = direction(B)  (magnitudes may differ)
formulas.hierarchy: Equal  ⊂  Parallel.   Every equal pair is parallel. Not every parallel pair is equal.

constraints: Equal vectors MUST match on both magnitude and direction
Parallel vectors need only match direction — magnitudes can differ
Position of a free vector is irrelevant for both equality and parallelism
Two vectors can be parallel with wildly different magnitudes (e.g., 1 m and 1000 m, both east)

STATE_1.title: Two Arrows Pointing the Same Way — Are They 'The Same'?

scene_composition.text:
Both arrows tilt the same way.
But one is 2 long, the other 5 long.

Are A and B the 'same' vector?
Or are they something else?

tts_sentences.text_en: Two arrows on the board. Both tilt the same way — both point thirty degrees above the horizontal.
One is short. One is long.
A student glances at this picture and says — they both point the same way, so they are the same vector.
That word 'same' is hiding two different ideas. Physics calls one pair parallel. The other equal. They are not interchangeable.

scene_composition.text:
Parallel vectors:
same direction.
Magnitude can be anything.

A, B, C all tilt 30° above x-axis.
Lengths: 2, 5, 7.
All three are parallel to each other.

tts_sentences.text_en: Parallel vectors share only one thing — the direction.
Three arrows, all tilted thirty degrees. Lengths two, five, seven. All parallel to each other.
Parallel doesn't care how long. It only cares which way.
Two trains on parallel tracks — one goods train crawling at twenty kilometres an hour, one express at a hundred. Their velocity vectors are parallel. They are clearly not equal.

STATE_3.title: Equal — Direction AND Magnitude. Position Doesn't Matter.

scene_composition.text:
Equal vectors:
same magnitude AND same direction.
Position on the page doesn't matter.

A = B = C.
All three represent the same vector,
just drawn in different places.

tts_sentences.text_en: Equal vectors match on two counts — direction AND magnitude.
Three arrows here. All tilt thirty degrees. All of length four. Different positions — top-left, centre, bottom-right.
Physics treats them as the same vector. Position is irrelevant for a free vector.
This property has a name — free vector. You can slide a vector anywhere on the page without changing it. Only magnitude and direction define the vector.
Position vectors and radius vectors are the only exception — those are fixed at the origin.

STATE_4.title: The Hierarchy — and the Classic JEE Trap

scene_composition.text:
Large circle: PARALLEL vectors (same direction)
  Smaller circle inside: EQUAL vectors (same direction AND same magnitude)

Every EQUAL pair is PARALLEL.
Not every PARALLEL pair is EQUAL.

JEE Assertion-Reason:
  Assertion:  Two equal vectors are always parallel.
  Reason:     Two parallel vectors are always equal.

  Assertion  → TRUE
  Reason     → FALSE
  Correct answer: Assertion true, Reason false.

tts_sentences.text_en: The relationship is a hierarchy. Parallel is the bigger set. Equal is a smaller set inside it.
Every equal pair is automatically parallel — because equal demands matching direction, and matching direction is exactly what parallel means.
But parallel does not imply equal. Parallel only says the directions match. Magnitudes are free to differ.
This is the classic assertion-reason trap in J-E-E. Assertion — equal vectors are parallel — TRUE. Reason — parallel vectors are equal — FALSE.
Mark the right option. Assertion correct, reason incorrect.

epic_c_branches.misconception: If two vectors are parallel they must be equal, and if equal they must be parallel — the two words mean the same thing
trigger_phrases: difference between equal and parallel vectors

STATE_1.title: Show the Belief — They Look the Same, So They Must Be the Same

scene_composition.text:
Student thinks:
   'Both point northeast.
    So they are the same vector.
    Equal = parallel.'

But |A| = 5 and |B| = 10.
Clearly not the same vector.

tts_sentences.text_en: The student sees two arrows both pointing northeast.
They reason — both point the same way, so they must be the same vector. Equal and parallel are just two words for the one idea.
But look — one has length five, the other length ten. They tilt the same way but they are not the same size.
In physics these two vectors are parallel, but they are definitely not equal.

STATE_2.title: The Hierarchy — Equal Is a Strict Subset

scene_composition.text:
PARALLEL  = same direction.
EQUAL     = same direction  AND  same magnitude.

Think of it as a Venn diagram:

   ┌─────── PARALLEL ──────────┐
   │                           │
   │    ┌── EQUAL ──┐           │
   │    │          │           │
   │    └──────────┘           │
   │                           │
   └───────────────────────────┘

Equal vectors sit inside the parallel circle.
But parallel includes many vectors
that are not equal.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the picture. Draw a large circle labelled parallel. Inside it draw a smaller circle labelled equal.
Every vector in the equal circle is also in the parallel circle. Equal is a strict subset of parallel.
But the parallel circle is larger — it contains vectors that are not equal to each other, just direction-matched.
If you remember this picture, you will never confuse the two again.

STATE_3.title: Free Vector — Why Position Is Irrelevant for Equality

scene_composition.text:
All three arrows
have magnitude 4
and direction 30°.

All three are EQUAL.
Position does not enter the definition.

A free vector can be translated anywhere —
it remains the same vector.

tts_sentences.text_en: One subtle point that trips students up. Position of the arrow is NOT part of vector equality.
Three arrows drawn at three different positions. All of length four, all tilted thirty degrees.
All three are EQUAL. They represent exactly the same vector A.
This is the free vector property. You can slide a vector anywhere on the page and it remains the same vector. Only magnitude and direction define it.

scene_composition.text:
JEE-style question:

   Assertion:  Two equal vectors are always parallel.
   Reason:     Two parallel vectors are always equal.

   Options:
   (a)  Both correct, Reason explains Assertion
   (b)  Both correct, Reason does NOT explain Assertion
   (c)  Assertion correct, Reason incorrect
   (d)  Assertion incorrect, Reason correct

Answer:  (c)  Assertion TRUE, Reason FALSE.

tts_sentences.text_en: This exact assertion-reason pattern shows up in J-E-E almost every year.
Assertion — two equal vectors are always parallel. Is that true? Yes. Equality demands matching direction, which is the very definition of parallel.
Reason — two parallel vectors are always equal. Is that true? No. Parallel only requires direction; magnitudes can differ.
Assertion is true. Reason is false. Mark option c.
If you remember the Venn diagram — equal is inside parallel, parallel is bigger — you will get this every single time.

regeneration_variants.label: Same concept, different magnitudes
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Parallel: two forces both acting north — one 10 N, one 30 N. Equal: two displacements both 5 m north from different starting points.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Equal = direction + magnitude; Parallel = direction only

regeneration_variants.label: Antiparallel sub-case
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Are antiparallel vectors parallel? The formal convention in physics — yes, they lie along the same line. They point opposite ways, but the line of action is the same.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Parallel allows 0° or 180° in some conventions; narrow convention restricts to 0° only
